title: States of Matter
subtitle: Chapter 2: Gases, Liquids and Solids — Complete NEB Notes

content.text: 1. Introduction to States of Matter
Matter exists in three primary states: Solid, Liquid, and Gas. The state depends on intermolecular forces, temperature, and pressure.
Gas: No fixed shape or volume; highly compressible; molecules move randomly with high kinetic energy.
Liquid: Fixed volume but no fixed shape; moderate intermolecular forces; flows easily.
Solid: Fixed shape and volume; strong intermolecular forces; particles vibrate about fixed positions.
2. Gas Laws
Gas laws describe the behavior of gases under varying conditions of pressure (P), volume (V), temperature (T), and amount (n).
2.1 Boyle's Law (1662)
At constant temperature and mass, the volume of a given mass of gas is inversely proportional to its pressure.

content.label: ⚠️ Important
content.text: Boyle's law applies only to ideal gases at constant temperature. Graph of P vs V is hyperbolic; P vs 1/V is straight line through origin.
2.2 Charles's Law
At constant pressure, the volume of a given mass of gas is directly proportional to its absolute temperature (in Kelvin).

content.name: Mathematical Form
content.note: For two states: V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂. Temperature must be in Kelvin: T(K) = t(°C) + 273

content.text: Absolute Zero: The theoretical temperature (-273.15°C or 0 K) at which the volume of an ideal gas becomes zero.
2.3 Combined Gas Law
Combining Boyle's and Charles's laws:

content.name: Combined Equation
content.note: Used when P, V, and T all change for a fixed mass of gas.

content.text: 2.4 Ideal Gas Equation
For n moles of an ideal gas:

content.name: Ideal Gas Law
content.note: R = Universal gas constant = 0.0821 L·atm·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹

content.text: Derivation: From Avogadro's law (V ∝ n), Boyle's law (V ∝ 1/P), Charles's law (V ∝ T) → V ∝ nT/P → PV = nRT

content.name: Density Form
content.note: Where M = molar mass, d = density (d = m/V)

content.text: 2.5 Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures
The total pressure exerted by a mixture of non-reacting gases is equal to the sum of the partial pressures of individual gases.

content.name: Mathematical Form
content.note: Partial pressure of gas i: Pᵢ = xᵢ × Pₜₒₜₐₗ, where xᵢ = mole fraction

content.label: 💡 Application
content.text: Used in collecting gases over water: P₍₉ₐₛ₎ = P₍ₜₒₜₐₗ₎ - P₍ᵥₐₚₒᵣ₎ (aqueous tension)
2.6 Graham's Law of Diffusion/Effusion
At constant T and P, the rate of diffusion/effusion of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of its molar mass.

content.name: Mathematical Form
content.note: r = rate of diffusion, M = molar mass, d = density. For time: t₁/t₂ = √(M₁/M₂)

content.text: Diffusion: Spreading of gas molecules throughout available space. Effusion: Escape of gas through a tiny hole.
3. Numerical Examples (Gas Laws)
Example 1: A gas occupies 200 mL at 760 mmHg. What volume at 950 mmHg (T constant)?
Example 2: Calculate molar mass of gas: 0.23 g occupies 126.4 mL at 760 mmHg and 27°C.
Example 3: Time for O₂ to diffuse vs H₂. M(O₂)=32, M(H₂)=2. If H₂ takes 5 min, O₂ takes?
4. Liquid State
Liquids have definite volume but no definite shape. Properties depend on intermolecular forces.
4.1 Vapour Pressure
The pressure exerted by vapours in equilibrium with their liquid at a given temperature in a closed container.
• Increases with temperature • Depends on nature of liquid (weaker forces → higher vapour pressure)
4.2 Boiling Point
The temperature at which the vapour pressure of liquid becomes equal to the atmospheric pressure.

content.label: 🌡️ Note
content.text: Boiling point decreases at high altitudes (lower atmospheric pressure).
4.3 Surface Tension
The force acting per unit length perpendicular to the surface of a liquid, tending to minimize surface area.

content.name: Unit
content.note: Causes spherical drops, capillary rise, insects walking on water

content.text: Cohesive force: Attraction between like molecules. Adhesive force: Attraction between unlike molecules.
4.4 Capillary Action
Rise or fall of liquid in a narrow tube due to adhesive and cohesive forces.
• Water in glass: concave meniscus (adhesive > cohesive) → rises • Mercury in glass: convex meniscus (cohesive > adhesive) → falls
4.5 Viscosity
The internal resistance to flow of a liquid. Due to friction between layers of liquid moving at different velocities.

content.name: Newton's Law of Viscosity
content.note: η = coefficient of viscosity; Unit: poise (1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s)

content.text: Viscosity decreases with increase in temperature (molecules gain kinetic energy, overcome intermolecular forces).

content.label: 🧪 Example
content.text: Glycerol > Water > Ethanol > Acetone (order of viscosity due to H-bonding strength)
5. Solid State
Solids have definite shape and volume due to strong intermolecular forces and fixed particle positions.
5.1 Crystalline vs Amorphous Solids
Crystalline Solids: Particles arranged in regular, repeating 3D pattern (long-range order). Sharp melting point, anisotropic. Examples: NaCl, diamond, quartz.
Amorphous Solids: Particles arranged randomly (short-range order). No sharp melting point, isotropic. Examples: glass, rubber, plastics.
Anisotropic: Physical properties (conductivity, refractive index) differ with direction. Isotropic: Same in all directions.
5.2 Water of Crystallization
Fixed number of water molecules loosely attached to one formula unit of a crystalline salt.
Examples: CuSO₄·5H₂O (blue vitriol), Na₂CO₃·10H₂O (washing soda), FeSO₄·7H₂O (green vitriol)
Efflorescence: Loss of water of crystallization to atmosphere (e.g., Na₂CO₃·10H₂O → Na₂CO₃·H₂O + 9H₂O).
Hygroscopic: Absorbs moisture but doesn't dissolve (e.g., conc. H₂SO₄, CaO). Deliquescent: Absorbs moisture and dissolves to form solution (e.g., NaOH, CaCl₂).
5.3 Crystal Lattice and Unit Cell
Crystal Lattice: Regular 3D arrangement of points representing constituent particles. Unit Cell: Smallest repeating unit that generates the entire lattice.
Types of Cubic Unit Cells:
1. Simple Cubic (SC): Particles only at corners. Coordination number = 6. Example: Polonium.
2. Body-Centred Cubic (BCC): Corners + one at body centre. CN = 8. Examples: Na, K, Fe.
3. Face-Centred Cubic (FCC): Corners + one at each face centre. CN = 12. Examples: Cu, Ag, Au, Al.
5.4 Seven Crystal Systems
Based on edge lengths (a, b, c) and interfacial angles (α, β, γ):

1. Cubic: a=b=c, α=β=γ=90° (NaCl, diamond) • 2. Tetragonal: a=b≠c, α=β=γ=90° (SnO₂) • 3. Orthorhombic: a≠b≠c, α=β=γ=90° (BaSO₄) • 4. Monoclinic: a≠b≠c, α=γ=90°, β≠90° (Na₂SO₄·10H₂O) • 5. Triclinic: a≠b≠c, α≠β≠γ≠90° (K₂Cr₂O₇) • 6. Trigonal/Rhombohedral: a=b=c, α=β=γ≠90° (Calcite) • 7. Hexagonal: a=b≠c, α=β=90°, γ=120° (Graphite, ZnO)
6. Important Formulas Summary

content.label: 🎯 Exam Tips
content.text: • Always convert temperature to Kelvin for gas laws • STP: 0°C (273 K) and 1 atm (760 mmHg) • Molar volume at STP = 22.4 L/mol • For diffusion problems, use molar mass in g/mol • Surface tension and viscosity decrease with temperature • Crystalline solids are anisotropic; amorphous are isotropic
7. Common Mistakes to Avoid

❌ Using °C instead of K in gas law calculations • ❌ Forgetting that Dalton's law applies only to non-reacting gases • ❌ Confusing diffusion (spreading) with effusion (through hole) • ❌ Assuming all solids with water are hydrated (check if water is in crystal lattice) • ❌ Thinking amorphous solids have no order (they have short-range order)
